text: During exploration, a previously placed tattoo mark is visible on the tissue surface.
During the intraoperative colonoscopy, the surgeon identifies the lesion within the colon.
The surgeon attempts to elevate the lesion using a submucosal injection of saline, but the elevation proves difficult, as previously noted.
The surgeon injects indocyanine green (ICG) to aid in the precise localization of the lesion during the minimally invasive robotic procedure.
The surgeon uses fluorescence imaging to precisely identify the anti-mesenteric border, corresponding to the location of the polyp, which is visibly highlighted.
The surgeon performs a longitudinal colotomy over the anti-mesenteric tinea, using the previously identified location of the polyp for precise incision placement.
Upon entering the colon, the surgeon confirms the location of the polyp using ICG fluorescence angiography, which highlights the polyp's position along the anti-mesenteric border.
The surgeon uses ICG fluorescence imaging to precisely locate the polyp within the submucosal plane of the colon.
The surgeon expands the colotomy along the longitudinal border of the anti-mesenteric colon, using fluorescence imaging for precise guidance.
The surgeon uses fluorescence imaging to precisely identify the entire lesion located at the anti-mesenteric border of the colon.
The surgeon elevates the lesion and excises it from the underlying muscle using monopolar energy and sharp dissection techniques.
The surgeon carefully excises the entire polyp, ensuring that the resection encompasses the polyp with gross negative borders along its entire length.
The surgeon uses the robotic platform to closely visualize and precisely maneuver scissors and a grasper to aid in the excision of the polyp.
The surgeon excises the lesion intact, ensuring that both the radial and deep margins are visibly negative.
After excising the polyp, the surgeon introduces a five millimeter endo bag through a robotic port to deliver and retrieve the excised polyp.
The surgeon prepares to close the polypectomy site after the excision of the polyp, ensuring the area is ready for closure.
The surgeon prepares to close the polypectomy site following a partial thickness excision, aiming to prevent thermal injury and perforation.
The surgeon uses a running continuous 3-0 V-Loc suture with a V-20 needle to close the excision site, ensuring secure approximation of the tissue edges.
The surgeon utilizes robotic instrumentation to perform suturing with consistent tissue bites and continuous motion, effectively maneuvering the suture through the excision site.
The surgeon gently pulls on the suture, lifting the tissue at the polypectomy site to facilitate closure.
The surgeon takes full thickness bites with the suture, carefully reloading the suture without dropping the needle, as part of the closure process at the polypectomy site.
The surgeon closes the defect from the polypectomy site to the corner using a continuous suturing technique, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated securely.
The surgeon uses robotic instrumentation to take additional full-thickness bites in a reverse fashion at the corner of the defect, ensuring the suture is securely locked.
The surgeon closes the colotomy by suturing the incision site, ensuring the tissue edges are approximated and secured.
The surgeon uses a 3-0 running V-lock suture to close the colotomy, employing a technique that maintains continuous motion and ensures the suture is locked securely.
The robotic platform efficiently maneuvers the suture, gently tucking it in while lifting and re-engaging the tissue. The needle tip is used to ensure the suture bite closes in a transverse fashion.
The surgeon uses the tip of the needle to position the suture squarely on the tissue, ensuring precise alignment during closure.
The surgeon demonstrates the repetitive suturing technique by introducing the suture through the tissue, grasping the tip with a grasper, reloading, and replacing the suture in a consistent manner.
The surgeon sutures the tissue in a zipper line fashion, using a running V-lock suture to efficiently close the colotomy.
The surgeon reaches the quarter of the defect using a running V-lock suture technique, continuing the precise placement of sutures along the tissue.
The surgeon reverses the motion of the robotic platform to lock the suture securely in place, ensuring the stitch is tight and stable.
The surgeon inspects the suture line for gaps and places interrupted vicryl sutures to reinforce the closure, ensuring a secure and continuous tissue approximation.
The surgeon performs a vinyl colonoscopy to visually confirm that the lumen is not narrowed, ensuring it is widely patent and the closure is appropriate.
